schools.summary: Joshua gathering all Israel to Shechem before God: 'and Joshua gathered all the tribes of Israel to Shechem, and called for the elders of Israel, and for their heads, and for their judges, and for their officers; and they presented themselves before God' — Joshua assembled all Israel at Shechem, the place of the patriarchs, before God (the and Joshua gathered all the tribes of Israel to Shechem, the gathering at Shechem, the and called for the elders of Israel, and for their heads, and for their judges, and for their officers, the leaders called, the and they presented themselves before God, before God, the figure, the solemn assembly at Shechem, the place of Abraham's altar and Jacob's covenant, before God, the truth that Joshua gathered the people to the place of the patriarchs to renew the covenant, Genesis 12:6, the figure of the people brought before God to hear his word and renew the covenant, the assembly at Shechem, the covenant renewal, vv.2); Joshua gathering all Israel to Shechem (the assembly, Genesis 12:6) — the gathering (to Shechem); 'they presented themselves before God' (the people before God to renew the covenant, the solemn presence, the figure, the people brought before God to hear his word, the providence, the assembly, the rehearsal of God's mercies to follow, vv.2) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And Joshua gathered all the tribes of Israel to Shechem, and called for the elders of Israel, and for their heads, and for their judges, and for their officers; and they presented themselves before God

schools.summary: That the fathers, even Terah and Abraham's house, served other gods beyond the river: 'and Joshua said unto all the people, Thus saith the LORD God of Israel, Your fathers dwelt on the other side of the flood in old time, even Terah, the father of Abraham, and the father of Nachor: and they served other gods' — the free grace of election — 'and Joshua said unto all the people, Thus saith the LORD God of Israel, Your fathers dwelt on the other side of the flood in old time, even Terah, the father of Abraham, and the father of Nachor: and they served other gods': Joshua, speaking God's word, declares that Abraham's own father and house were idolaters (the witnesses noting the significance — the free and sovereign grace of God, who called Abraham not because he or his fathers were worshippers of the true God, but out of idolatry itself, the line of promise drawn from a house that served other gods, Terah and his family idolaters in Ur and beyond the Euphrates, Abraham called by pure grace, 'I took your father Abraham from the other side of the flood,' Joshua 24:3; Genesis 11:31, the lesson — that God's election rests on his own free mercy and not on any worthiness in those he chooses, the people reminded that their very origin was idolatry, lest they boast in themselves, 'not by works of righteousness which we have done, but according to his mercy he saved us,' Titus 3:5, the truth that grace finds men in their sin and calls them out, the root of Israel an idolater rescued by God, 'such were some of you: but ye are washed,' 1 Corinthians 6:11, the figure — the calling of the sinner out of darkness by sovereign grace, Abraham a pattern of the elect drawn from idolatry, the truth that all boasting is excluded, the people owing all to God's free choice, the figure of the free electing grace of God, who calls his own out of idolatry by no merit of theirs, the call to humility and gratitude in those chosen by grace, the warning against boasting in any worthiness of our own, the figure of the free electing grace of God, who called Abraham out of idolatry); Joshua rehearsing that the fathers, Terah and his house, dwelt beyond the Euphrates in old time, before God called Abraham (the origin beyond the river, Genesis 11:31) — the fathers (beyond the flood); 'and they served other gods' (the fathers, even Abraham's house, served other gods, the line of promise drawn from idolatry by God's free grace, election resting on mercy and not worthiness, the free grace of election, Titus 3:5; 1 Corinthians 6:11) — the figure (the calling of the sinner out of darkness by sovereign grace, the providence, the origin, God's taking and leading of Abraham to follow, vv.3) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the rehearsal that the fathers served other gods establish?

sides.position: The free electing grace of God, who called Abraham out of idolatry by no merit of his own - the line of promise drawn from a house of idolaters (Genesis 11:31), God's election resting on his own free mercy and not on any worthiness (Titus 3:5), grace finding men in their sin and calling them out (1 Corinthians 6:11), that all boasting be excluded

themes: And Joshua said unto all the people, Thus saith the LORD God of Israel, Your fathers dwelt on the other side of the flood in old time, even Terah, the father of Abraham, and the father of Nachor: and they served other gods
that the fathers, even Terah and Abraham's house, served other gods beyond the river (the crux of the free electing grace of God, who called Abraham out of idolatry)
the free and sovereign grace of God, who called Abraham not for any worthiness but out of idolatry itself, the line of promise drawn from a house of idolaters
God's election resting on his own free mercy and not on merit, that all boasting be excluded (Genesis 11:31; Titus 3:5; 1 Corinthians 6:11)

schools.summary: God taking Abraham, leading him, and giving him Isaac: 'and I took your father Abraham from the other side of the flood, and led him throughout all the land of Canaan, and multiplied his seed, and gave him Isaac' — God took Abraham from beyond the river, led him through Canaan, and gave him Isaac (the and I took your father Abraham from the other side of the flood, God took Abraham, the and led him throughout all the land of Canaan, and multiplied his seed, and gave him Isaac, led him and gave Isaac, the figure, God's free grace taking and leading Abraham, the work all God's, the truth that God took, led, multiplied, and gave, the verbs all of grace, Genesis 12:1, the figure of the sovereign grace that calls, leads, and blesses, 'I took... I led... I gave,' the work of God in the patriarch, the grace of election, vv.2); God taking and leading Abraham (the grace, Genesis 12:1) — the taking (from the flood); 'multiplied his seed, and gave him Isaac' (God multiplying the seed and giving Isaac, the grace, the figure, the sovereign grace that calls, leads, and blesses, the providence, the grace, Jacob and Esau to follow, vv.4) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: God giving Jacob and Esau, Esau Seir, and Jacob going to Egypt: 'and I gave unto Isaac Jacob and Esau: and I gave unto Esau mount Seir, to possess it; but Jacob and his children went down into Egypt' — God gave Isaac two sons, gave Esau Seir, and Jacob went to Egypt (the and I gave unto Isaac Jacob and Esau, Jacob and Esau given, the and I gave unto Esau mount Seir, to possess it, Esau given Seir, the but Jacob and his children went down into Egypt, Jacob to Egypt, the figure, God's choice of Jacob and the descent to Egypt, the line of promise, the truth that God gave Esau his portion but kept Jacob for the promise, the descent to Egypt the way to the nation, Genesis 46:6, the figure of the electing grace that distinguishes Jacob from Esau, 'Jacob have I loved, but Esau have I hated,' Romans 9:13, the choice of Jacob, the line of promise, vv.3); God giving Jacob and Esau, and Esau Seir (the distinction, Romans 9:13) — the sons (Jacob and Esau); 'Jacob and his children went down into Egypt' (Jacob's descent to Egypt, the line of promise, the providence, Genesis 46:6, the figure, the electing grace that distinguishes Jacob from Esau, the providence, the descent, Moses and the deliverance to follow, vv.5) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: God sending Moses and Aaron, plaguing Egypt, and bringing Israel out: 'I sent Moses also and Aaron, and I plagued Egypt, according to that which I did among them: and afterward I brought you out' — God sent Moses and Aaron, plagued Egypt, and brought Israel out (the I sent Moses also and Aaron, and I plagued Egypt, according to that which I did among them, Moses sent, Egypt plagued, the and afterward I brought you out, brought out, the figure, God's mighty work in the deliverance from Egypt, the redemption of the nation, the truth that God sent his servants, smote Egypt, and brought Israel out, Exodus 3:10, the figure of the redemption wrought by God's power, 'I brought you out,' the deliverance from Egypt, the work of God, vv.4); God sending Moses and plaguing Egypt (the work, Exodus 3:10) — the plagues (Egypt smitten); 'afterward I brought you out' (God bringing Israel out, the redemption, the figure, the redemption wrought by God's power, the providence, the deliverance, the Red Sea crossing to follow, vv.6-7) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: God bringing the fathers out and the Egyptians pursuing to the Red Sea: 'and I brought your fathers out of Egypt: and ye came unto the sea; and the Egyptians pursued after your fathers with chariots and horsemen unto the Red sea' — God brought the fathers out, and the Egyptians pursued to the Red Sea (the and I brought your fathers out of Egypt: and ye came unto the sea, brought to the sea, the and the Egyptians pursued after your fathers with chariots and horsemen unto the Red sea, the pursuit, the figure, the fathers at the sea, the foe pursuing, the danger before deliverance, the truth that Israel came to the sea with the foe behind, the strait that God would open, Exodus 14:9, the figure of the danger that precedes God's deliverance, the foe pursuing, the pursuit to the Red Sea, the strait before the wonder, vv.5); the fathers brought to the sea (the strait) — the coming (to the sea); 'the Egyptians pursued after your fathers with chariots and horsemen unto the Red sea' (the foe pursuing to the sea, the danger, Exodus 14:9, the figure, the danger that precedes God's deliverance, the providence, the pursuit, the darkness and the sea covering the Egyptians to follow, vv.7) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: God covering the Egyptians in the sea and keeping Israel in the wilderness: 'and when they cried unto the LORD, he put darkness between you and the Egyptians, and brought the sea upon them, and covered them; and your eyes have seen what I have done in Egypt: and ye dwelt in the wilderness a long season' — God answered their cry, drowned the Egyptians, and kept Israel in the wilderness (the and when they cried unto the LORD, he put darkness between you and the Egyptians, and brought the sea upon them, and covered them, the Egyptians drowned, the and your eyes have seen what I have done in Egypt: and ye dwelt in the wilderness a long season, the wilderness years, the figure, God answering the cry, drowning the foe, and keeping the people, the deliverance and the care, the truth that God heard their cry, covered the Egyptians, and sustained them in the wilderness, Exodus 14:30, the figure of the God who delivers from the foe and sustains in the wilderness, the deliverance and the wilderness, the care of God, vv.6); God covering the Egyptians in the sea (the deliverance, Exodus 14:30) — the drowning (the sea covered them); 'ye dwelt in the wilderness a long season' (the long years in the wilderness, the care, the figure, the God who delivers and sustains, the providence, the wilderness, the conquest of the Amorites to follow, vv.8) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: God sending the hornet, the conquest not by Israel's own sword: 'and I sent the hornet before you, which drave them out from before you, even the two kings of the Amorites; but not with thy sword, nor with thy bow' — God sent the hornet to drive out the foe, the victory not by Israel's own strength (the and I sent the hornet before you, which drave them out from before you, even the two kings of the Amorites, the hornet drove them out, the but not with thy sword, nor with thy bow, not by their sword, the figure, God driving out the foe by the hornet, not by Israel's strength, the victory wholly his, the truth that the conquest was God's work and not Israel's sword, Exodus 23:28, the figure of the victory that is not by man's strength but by God, 'not by might, nor by power, but by my spirit,' Zechariah 4:6, the conquest not by the sword, the gift of God, vv.11); God sending the hornet to drive out the foe (the gift, Exodus 23:28) — the hornet (drove them out); 'not with thy sword, nor with thy bow' (the victory not by Israel's strength, the gift, Zechariah 4:6, the figure, the victory not by man's strength but by God, the providence, the gift, the land given for which they laboured not to follow, vv.13) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: God giving Israel a land for which they laboured not: 'and I have given you a land for which ye did not labour, and cities which ye built not, and ye dwell in them; of the vineyards and oliveyards which ye planted not do ye eat' — God gave Israel a land, cities, and vineyards they had not laboured for (the and I have given you a land for which ye did not labour, and cities which ye built not, and ye dwell in them, the land and cities unearned, the of the vineyards and oliveyards which ye planted not do ye eat, the vineyards unearned, the figure, the inheritance wholly of grace, unearned and unlaboured for, the gift of God, the truth that Israel received the land, cities, and vineyards by gift, not by their own labour, Deuteronomy 6:10-11, the figure of the unearned inheritance of grace, the believer enjoying what he did not earn, 'ye are saved through faith... not of works,' the gift of God, the grace of the inheritance, vv.12); the land and cities given unearned (the grace, Deuteronomy 6:10-11) — the gift (land not laboured for); 'of the vineyards and oliveyards which ye planted not do ye eat' (the vineyards enjoyed unearned, the grace, the figure, the unearned inheritance of grace, the providence, the gift, the call to fear and serve the LORD to follow, vv.14) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Joshua's charge to choose this day whom they would serve, and his own resolve for himself and his house: 'and if it seem evil unto you to serve the LORD, choose you this day whom ye will serve; whether the gods which your fathers served that were on the other side of the flood, or the gods of the Amorites, in whose land ye dwell: but as for me and my house, we will serve the LORD' — the decisive choice — 'and if it seem evil unto you to serve the LORD, choose you this day whom ye will serve... but as for me and my house, we will serve the LORD': Joshua sets the choice before the people and declares his own settled resolve (the witnesses noting the significance — the necessity of a decisive, personal, present choice between the LORD and all idols, no neutrality possible, the service of God a deliberate act of the will, the choice set plainly, 'this day,' the people pressed to decide for God or against him, the heart that will not serve God will serve idols, the lesson — that every man must choose whom he will serve, there is no middle ground, and the choice must be made now, 'how long halt ye between two opinions? if the LORD be God, follow him,' 1 Kings 18:21, the truth that the godly leader takes his own stand whatever others do, resolving for himself and his house to serve the LORD, the head of the household leading his family in godliness, 'but as for me and my house, we will serve the LORD,' the figure — the personal commitment of faith that does not wait on the multitude, the believer's resolve to serve God though all forsake him, 'though all men shall be offended... yet will not I,' Mark 14:29, the truth that the master of a house is bound to lead his household in the service of God, the family religion that flows from the leader's resolve, 'command his children and his household after him,' Genesis 18:19, the figure of the decisive choice and the leader's personal commitment, the call to choose the LORD this day and to lead one's house in his service, the warning that not to choose God is to choose idols, the figure of the decisive choice between God and idols and the leader's personal commitment whatever others do); Joshua pressing the people to a present and decisive choice between the LORD and the idols of their fathers or the Amorites, no neutrality allowed (the decisive choice, 1 Kings 18:21) — the choice (whom ye will serve); 'but as for me and my house, we will serve the LORD' (Joshua declaring his own settled resolve for himself and his house, the godly leader taking his stand whatever others do and leading his family in God's service, the personal commitment, Mark 14:29; Genesis 18:19) — the figure (the believer's resolve to serve God though all forsake him, the providence, the choice, the people's answer to follow, vv.16) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does Joshua's charge 'choose you this day' and 'as for me and my house' establish?

sides.position: The decisive choice between God and idols and the leader's personal commitment whatever others do - the necessity of a deliberate, personal, present choice with no neutrality possible (1 Kings 18:21), the godly leader taking his own stand though all forsake him (Mark 14:29) and bound to lead his household in the service of God (Genesis 18:19)

the mercies that ground their resolve to serve him (Exodus 20:2)

schools.summary: The people resolving to serve the LORD, for he is their God: 'and the LORD drave out from before us all the people, even the Amorites which dwelt in the land: therefore will we also serve the LORD; for he is our God' — the people, owning God's driving out the nations, resolved to serve him (the and the LORD drave out from before us all the people, even the Amorites which dwelt in the land, God drove out the nations, the therefore will we also serve the LORD; for he is our God, resolve to serve him, the figure, the people's resolve grounded on God's mercies, he is our God, the truth that the driving out of the nations grounds the resolve to serve the LORD, the mercies the motive, Psalm 100:3, the figure of the resolve to serve God grounded on his grace, 'he is our God,' the resolve to serve, the profession of loyalty, vv.17); God driving out the nations (the mercy, Psalm 100:3) — the driving out (the nations); 'therefore will we also serve the LORD; for he is our God' (the resolve to serve God, for he is theirs, the profession, the figure, the resolve to serve God grounded on his grace, the providence, the resolve, Joshua's solemn warning to follow, vv.19) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And the LORD drave out from before us all the people, even the Amorites which dwelt in the land: therefore will we also serve the LORD; for he is our God
the people resolving to serve the LORD, for he is their God

the resolve to serve God grounded on his grace (Psalm 100:3)
